title: Replace mutable order row with an event-sourced Order aggregate and projection
business_context: A logistics startup processes 220k orders/day. The orders table is updated in place across 6 microservices; nightly disputes ('who set status=cancelled?') cost the ops team ~12h/week of forensic log-grepping. There is no reliable audit trail and concurrent writes silently clobber each other.
constraints: Must keep existing GET /orders/:id contract byte-identical for 40+ downstream consumers
Postgres only, no Kafka in scope this quarter
Optimistic concurrency: two services may act on same order within 50ms
Projections must be rebuildable from scratch in < 30min

before_state.description: Orders are a single mutable row; services issue UPDATE orders SET status=... with last-write-wins and no version check.

issues: Concurrent cancel + ship races: final status is nondeterministic
No history: cancel_reason overwrites previous, audit impossible
No concurrency error surfaced to caller
Response not derived from authoritative state

reasoning: Naive approach: add an updated_by column and a trigger-based audit table. Rejected: triggers capture rows not intent, so we still cannot replay 'why' and cannot enforce transition rules; audit table also doubles write amplification under the 3k/s target. Chosen path traces UI(cancel button)->API(POST /cancel carries expectedVersion)->domain(Order.cancel() validates current state, emits OrderCancelled)->data(append to order_events with unique (order_id, version) constraint giving optimistic concurrency for free)->async(a projector consumes the outbox and upserts order_read)->config(feature flag es_orders routes reads to projection)->tests(replay equivalence: projection state == folded events)->rollout(dual-write then cutover).

after_state.description: Order is an aggregate folded from an append-only event log; reads served by a rebuildable projection; concurrency enforced by a unique version constraint.

files.purpose: Pure decision + fold functions; transition rules enforced before any event is emitted.
Append-only store with version-collision detection mapping 23505 to a 409.
Read-decide-append flow; surfaces 409 on concurrency and 422 on illegal transition.
Idempotent projection; version guard makes replay/reprocessing safe.

migration_strategy: Create order_events + order_read tables; deploy projector reading nothing yet (no-op)
Dual-write: API appends events AND keeps legacy UPDATE behind flag es_write=on
Backfill: synthesize OrderPlaced/Shipped/Cancelled from legacy rows into order_events, then rebuild order_read
Flip reads to order_read behind es_read flag at 1% -> 10% -> 100% comparing against legacy row
Stop legacy UPDATEs (es_write only); keep legacy table read-only for 2 weeks
Drop legacy columns/triggers after parity dashboard is green for 14 days
testing_strategy: Fixture seeds 3 concurrent requests (cancel, ship, cancel) against one order via Promise.all on real Postgres; assert exactly one succeeds with 200, others get 409/422, and order_events has no duplicate versions. Side-effect asserts: order_read.version equals fold(events).version and status matches. Chaos test: kill projector mid-batch, restart, assert projection converges to the same state (idempotent replay) with zero duplicate rows.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Last-write-wins UPDATE: a cancel and a ship arriving within the 50ms window both succeed, producing a 'shipped' order that was also 'cancelled' with no record of ordering — exactly the dispute the ops team cannot resolve.

title: Introduce snapshots so a 40k-event wallet aggregate stops timing out on load
business_context: A fintech wallet service event-sources every balance change. Power-merchant wallets accumulate 40k+ events over 18 months; loading one to authorize a payment now folds the full stream and takes 1.8s p99, breaching the 300ms auth SLA and causing 0.4% of payments to time out at checkout.
constraints: Snapshots must never produce a balance different from full replay
Schema of events may evolve; snapshots must be versioned
No double-counting if a snapshot lands concurrently with new appends
Must support point-in-time replay for audits ignoring snapshots
Redis available for snapshot cache, Postgres is source of truth

before_state.description: Every wallet load replays the entire event stream from version 1.

issues: No upper bound on stream length
p99 grows unbounded as merchant ages

reasoning: Naive approach: cache the materialized balance in Redis and trust it. Rejected: a cached scalar cannot answer point-in-time audit queries and goes wrong on cache/db skew with no way to detect drift. Chosen: versioned snapshots persisted in Postgres (auditable) + a Redis read-through cache. Trace: API(authorize)->load(read latest snapshot <= current, fold only the tail events)->snapshotter(async job writes snapshot every N events with snapshot.version captured atomically)->config(SNAPSHOT_EVERY=500)->tests(snapshot+tail fold == full fold)->rollout(shadow-compare snapshot balance vs full replay before trusting).

after_state.description: Load reads the newest snapshot then folds only events after it; snapshots are versioned and verifiable.

files.purpose: Bounded load: snapshot base + only the tail folds, keeping work O(SNAPSHOT_EVERY).
Idempotent snapshot write keyed by version so concurrent appends never double-write.
Forward-migrates old snapshot shapes so schema evolution never invalidates stored snapshots.

migration_strategy: Deploy snapshotter writing snapshots but keep loadWallet on full replay (snapshots unused)
Shadow mode: load both ways, log mismatch metric wallet_snapshot_drift
After 48h of zero drift, switch load to snapshot+tail behind flag snap_read
Ramp snap_read 5%->50%->100% watching p99 and drift
Backfill snapshots for hot wallets (top 1% by event count) via batch job
Remove full-replay fallback once p99<300ms holds for 7 days (audit path keeps full replay)
testing_strategy: Fixture builds a wallet with 1200 events (crosses two snapshots); assert loadWallet == fold(all events) exactly. Side-effect assert: exactly 2 snapshot rows exist at versions 500 and 1000. Chaos test: append event v1201 concurrently while snapshotter runs at v1000; assert no balance double-count and snapshot table has no duplicate (wallet_id,version).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting a Redis scalar: on a cache eviction during a deploy the service reads 0 balance and authorizes an overdraft; there's no event tail to reconcile against, so the error is silent until settlement.

title: Make a Kafka projection idempotent to survive at-least-once redelivery
business_context: A social platform projects PostLiked/PostUnliked events from Kafka into a Postgres like_counts table powering the feed. After a consumer-group rebalance, Kafka redelivers ~3k events; like counts drift upward (a post showed 2.1M likes vs the true 1.9M) and a viral creator publicly complained, triggering an incident.
constraints: Exactly-once effect on a counter despite at-least-once delivery
Counter reads must stay strongly consistent within a partition
No distributed transaction across Kafka and Postgres
Reprocessing a partition from offset 0 must converge to the same counts
Per-post ordering guaranteed by partition key = post_id

before_state.description: Projector applies +1/-1 deltas with no dedupe; redelivery double-applies.

issues: DB write and offset commit not atomic -> gap or overlap on crash
No record of which events were applied

reasoning: Naive approach: store the last committed offset in Kafka and rely on commit ordering. Rejected: the DB write and Kafka offset commit are two systems with no shared transaction, so any crash between them double-applies or skips. Chosen: track processed event ids in the SAME Postgres transaction as the projection update, making the effect idempotent and the offset advisory. Trace: consumer(poll batch)->projector(begin tx; INSERT id into processed_events ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING; if inserted, apply delta; commit)->offset(commit to Kafka after tx; redelivery is now harmless)->config(dedupe table partitioned by post_id hash)->tests(replay from 0 yields identical counts)->rollout(rebuild counts from log once dedupe is in place).

after_state.description: Projection update and a processed-id insert share one Postgres transaction; offsets become a performance hint, not a correctness mechanism.

files.purpose: Idempotent apply: dedupe insert and counter update in one tx; redelivery is a no-op.
Hash-partitioned dedupe table sized for 25k/s with a retention job dropping old partitions.
Deterministic rebuild from the log proving the projection is a pure function of events.

migration_strategy: Ship processed_events table + dedupe code in shadow (write dedupe rows, still old counter path)
Rebuild like_counts in a staging table from offset 0 using the idempotent path
Diff staging counts vs live; investigate any post with |delta|>0
Cut feed reads to the rebuilt table behind flag idempotent_likes
Decommission old non-idempotent projector; keep rebuild job for DR
Add retention cron dropping processed_events partitions older than 7 days
testing_strategy: Fixture publishes 1000 like/unlike events, then re-publishes the same 1000 (simulating rebalance redelivery); assert like_counts equals the single-application result and processed_events has exactly 1000 rows. Side-effect assert: re-running onBatch on the same batch performs zero counter updates (spy on UPDATE). Chaos test: throw after dedupe insert but before counter update inside the tx; assert the tx rolls back so neither dedupe row nor counter change persists, and redelivery applies it exactly once.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Committing the offset separately from the DB write: a crash after the +1 but before the offset commit replays the event on restart, adding a second +1 — the exact source of the 2.1M-vs-1.9M drift.
